content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

December 2, 1999


Ted W. Dutton
United Water Management Co., Inc.
1905 Business Center Drive, Suite 101
San Bernardino, California 92408

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-99-274

Dear Mr. Dutton:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the post-governmental employment restrictions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

I. QUESTION

As a former executive officer for the Office of Public School Construction and the State Allocation Board, may you assist school districts in understanding the new regulations that were written by the Office of Public School Construction and approved by the State Allocation Board, as long as you do not personally appear before or communicate with either your former agency or the board for one year after leaving office?

II. CONCLUSION

The Act's one-year ban prohibiting specified state officials from lobbying his or her former agency does not restrict you from providing this assistance. In addition, the permanent ban would not apply to you as long as the work you intend to perform does not involve an application that was processed while you were in state service.

___________________________

¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91015. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 - 18996, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-99-274
Page 2

III. FACTS

You were a former executive officer for both the Office of Public School Construction
and the State Allocation Board. You previously asked this office for assistance regarding the
revolving door restrictions. The facts in your letter dated August 23, 1999, stated that the duties
of your previous position included processing grant money applications for school modification,
and new school construction. Your office presented the applications monthly to the members of
the State Allocation Board, which then allocated the funds to schools and districts.

In our telephone conversation on November 22, 1999, you provided additional facts. You
stated that you left state employment on August 1, 1999. You are currently a consultant for
United Strategies whose primary responsibility is governmental relations with cities. You are
also the Executive Vice-President of United Water Management Company.

You also indicated that SB 50 was enacted at the end of last year and that the new law
provides $9.2 billion in funding over four years. Approximately $6.7 billion of that funding is
appropriated for K-12 schools. One thousand school districts (of which 300 are very small
school districts) and 8,000 schools are eligible to apply for funding. You were at the agency
when it adopted the emergency regulations implementing the new legislation.

Small school districts have asked you to assist them in understanding the new regulations
so that they may apply for funding. You would be reimbursed for your expenses and your
employer, United Strategies, would be paid for your services as a consultant. Your services
would not include soliciting or lobbying either the Office of Public Construction or the State
Allocation Board on behalf of these school districts.

IV. ANALYSIS

The Act's post-governmental restrictions limit the types of contacts a former employee
may have with his or her former agency.

One-Year Ban

Section 87406(d)(1) of the Act provides that no officer or designated employee of a state
administrative agency:

"[F]or a period of one year after leaving office or employment, shall, for
compensation, act as agent or attorney for, or otherwise represent, any
other person, by making any formal or informal appearance, or by making
any oral or written communication, before any state administrative agency,
or officer or employee thereof, for which he or she worked or represented
during the 12 months before leaving office or employment, if the appearance or

File No. A-99-274
Page 3

communication is made for the purpose of influencing2 administrative or
legislative action,3 or influencing any action or proceeding involving the
issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant,
or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. (Emphasis added.)

Under this section, representation must be for compensation in connection with
representation of another person. (Regulation 18746.1(b)(3) and (4).) The Act defines "person"
as any "individual, proprietorship, firm, partnership, joint venture, syndicate, business trust,
company, corporation, limited liability company, association, committee, and any other
organization or group of persons acting in concert." (Section 82047.) Thus, this definition of
"person" includes local public agencies within its scope. (In re Witt (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 1.)
Therefore, you may not represent a school district by appearing before either the Office of Public
Construction or the State Allocation Board for the purpose of influencing administrative or
legislative action or any proceeding involving the awarding of a grant. (Section 87406(d)(1).)

An appearance or written communication is for the purpose of influencing if it is "made
for the principal purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying,
or advancing the action or proceeding." (Regulation 18746.2.) Thus, communications that are
not for the purpose of influencing administrative or legislative action or a proceeding enumerated
in Section 87406(d)(1) are not restricted by the one-year ban. For example, an ex-employee can
attend informational meetings with the agency, or request information from the agency
concerning existing laws, regulations, or policies, so long as the employee does not attempt to
influence administrative or legislative action. (Regulation 18202(a)(1).) Further, the
Commission has advised that a former agency official may draft proposals on a client's behalf to
be submitted to the agency so long as the former employee is not identified in connection with
the client's efforts to influence the agency. (Davidian Advice Letter, No. A-97-076a.) Similarly,
the ex-employee may use his or her expertise to advise clients on the procedural requirements,
plans, or policies of the employee's former agency so long as the employee is not identified with
the employer's efforts to influence the agency.

2 "Influencing legislative or administrative action" includes influencing by any means, including but not
limited to, the provision or use of information, statistics, or analyses. (Section 82032.)

3 "Administrative action" is defined as the proposal, drafting, development, consideration, amendment,
enactment or defeat by any state agency of any rule, regulation or other action in any rate-making proceeding or any
quasi-legislative proceeding. (Section 82002.)

3 Section 82037 defines "legislative action" as the drafting, introduction, consideration, modification,
enactment or defeat of any bill, resolution, amendment, report, nomination or other matter by the Legislature or by
either house or any committee, subcommittee, joint or select committee thereof, or by a member or employee of the
Legislature acting in his or her official capacity. "Legislative action" also means the action of the Governor in
approving or vetoing a bill.

File No. A-99-274
Page 4

Certain other informal contacts may not be considered influencing. As we discussed in
our telephone conversation on November 22, 1999, for example, an ex-employee may request
information concerning anything that is a matter of public record, such as existing laws,
regulations, or policies. (Section 18746.2.) In addition, social conversations are not considered
influencing if the conversation is not intended to influence administrative or legislative action or
any other action specified in Section 87406. (Davidian Advice Letter, supra.)

You indicated to me that you will be assisting the districts with interpreting the new
regulations and completing grant money application forms. Your assistance will not include
appearing before or communicating with the Office of Public Construction or the State
Allocation Board for the purpose of influencing the agency with regard to the grant. Therefore,
as long as you are not identified in connection with these efforts, the one-year ban does not
prohibit you from providing this assistance.

Permanent Ban on "Switching Sides"

Sections 87401 and 87402 (commonly referred to as the "permanent ban") provide
additional restrictions on the activity of former state officials that may apply even where Section
87406 does not:

"No former state administrative official, after the termination of his
or her employment or term of office, shall for compensation act as
agent or attorney for, or otherwise represent, any other person (other
than the State of California) before any court or state administrative
agency or any officer or employee thereof by making any formal or
informal appearance, or by making any oral or written communication
with the intent to influence, in connection with any judicial, quasi-
judicial or other proceeding if both of the following apply:
(a) The State of California is a party or has a direct and substantial
interest.
(b) The proceeding is one in which the former state administrative
official participated." (Section 87401.)

"No former state administrative official, after the termination of his
or her employment or term of office shall for compensation aid, advise,
counsel, consult or assist in representing any other person (except the
State of California) in any proceeding in which the official would be
prohibited from appearing under Section 87401." (Section 87402.)

File No. A-99-274
Page 5

The permanent ban of Sections 87401 and 87402 applies only to judicial, quasi-judicial,
or other proceedings before any court or state administrative agency in which a former employee
participated while at his or her former agency. Section 87400(c) defines "judicial, quasi-judicial
or other proceeding" as:

"[A]ny proceeding, application, request for a ruling or other determination,
contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, arrest
or other particular matter involving a specific party or parties in any court
or state administrative agency, including but not limited to any proceeding
governed by Chapter 5 (commencing with Section 11500) of Division 3 of
Title 2 of the Government Code."

Under Section 87400(c) set forth above, a "proceeding" includes any particular matter
involving a specific party or parties in a state administrative agency. Proceedings that are
regulatory or of a general nature are not "proceedings" for purposes of Section 87401 since there
are no specific parties involved.

An official is considered to have "participated" in a proceeding if he or she took part in
the proceeding "personally and substantially through decision, approval, disapproval, formal
written recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation or use of
confidential information." (Section 87400(d).) This covers any proceeding in which any
employee has actually participated while at his or her former agency, as well as any proceeding
which he or she supervised. (Regulation 18741.1(a)(4).)

You stated that the work you would perform for the schools would be assisting them with
the new regulations and new forms that are required for the schools to apply for the new funding
that is now available. As long as the work you intend to perform does not involve an application
that was processed while you were in state service, the permanent ban would not apply to you.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at
(916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
Assistant General Counsel

By: Lynda Doherty
Political Reform Consultant, Legal Division

LM:LD:klw

written by the Office of Public School Construction and approved by the State Allocation Board,
as long as you do not personally appear before or communicate with either your former agency
or the board for one year after leaving office?

CONCLUSION: The Act’s one-year ban prohibiting specified state officials from lobbying his or her
former agency does not restrict you from providing this assistance. In addition, the permanent
ban would not apply to you as long as the work you intend to perform does not involve an
application that was processed while you were in state service.Page 2
Ill. FACTS
You were a former executive officer for both the Office of Public School Construction

Small school districts have asked you to assist them in understanding the new regulations
